title: From Branches to Bytes: Extending Learned Static Prediction to Data Layout Optimization
description: We extend Rotem & Cummins' proven methodology of 'learned static prediction' from branch probabilities to data layout optimization. By training lightweight XGBoost models on LLVM IR features (loop nesting, access patterns, field affinity), we predict profitable structure layout transformations without profiling. This work positions itself as a domain extension of Rotem & Cummins (2021) to the harder problem of data layout, distinct from RebaseDL's static analysis approach.
motivation: Data layout optimization can significantly improve cache performance, but production compilers have abandoned automatic optimization due to: (1) varying access patterns across code regions, (2) imprecise static analysis for identifying hot fields, and (3) the overhead of profile-guided optimization. Rotem & Cummins (2021) proved that ML can eliminate profiling for branch prediction; we extend this proven methodology to data layout, where no prior learned static prediction work exists. Current static approaches (RebaseDL) use conservative analysis that misses opportunities ML might capture.
proposed_approach: We apply Rotem & Cummins' methodology to data layout: (1) Feature extraction from LLVM IR - capturing loop nesting, field access patterns, control flow, and pointer usage (different features than branch prediction); (2) Lightweight ML models - using XGBoost for fast inference, trained on labeled optimal layouts; (3) Transformation prediction - identifying split/reorder candidates and expected profitability; (4) Validation through alias analysis. Unlike RebaseDL's static analysis with fixed thresholds, ML learns patterns from data. Unlike prior profile-based approaches, no runtime profiling is needed.

related_work.paper: Profile Guided Optimization without Profiles: A Machine Learning Approach (Rotem & Cummins, 2021)
related_work.difference: Foundational work proving learned static prediction works for branch probabilities using XGBoost on LLVM IR. Our work EXTENDS this proven methodology to data layout, extracting different features and predicting layout decisions rather than branch probabilities.
Classic static heuristics for branch prediction. We target data layout which is inherently harder (higher-dimensional output, context-dependent, legality constraints).

related_work.paper: Static Branch Frequency and Program Profile Analysis (Wu & Larus, 1994)
related_work.difference: Static estimation of branch frequencies. We apply similar ideas to field access frequency estimation for layout decisions.

related_work.authors: Trishul M. Chilimbi, Bob Davidson, James R. Larus
related_work.difference: Profile-guided structure splitting. We eliminate profiling requirement through learned static prediction.

related_work.paper: Array Regrouping and Structure Splitting using Whole-program Reference Affinity (Zhong et al., 2004)
related_work.authors: Yutao Zhong, Maksim Orlovich, Xipeng Shen, Chen Ding
related_work.difference: Uses profile-based reference affinity analysis. Our approach uses static ML prediction instead of profiling.

related_work.paper: Region-Based Data Layout via Data Reuse Analysis (Rohwedder et al., 2024)
related_work.authors: Caio S. Rohwedder, João P. L. De Carvalho, J. Nelson Amaral
related_work.difference: RebaseDL uses STATIC ANALYSIS with manually-tuned thresholds for structure splitting. We use MACHINE LEARNING to learn prediction rules from data. ML may discover patterns missed by conservative static analysis. Approaches are complementary.
Comprehensive thesis on region-based data layout. Our work specifically targets the prediction component, using ML where RebaseDL uses static heuristics.

related_work.paper: MLGO: a Machine Learning Guided Compiler Optimizations Framework (Trofin et al., 2021)
related_work.difference: Uses RL for inlining/register allocation. We target data layout specifically and use supervised learning (XGBoost) rather than RL.

related_work.paper: LOOPer: A Learned Automatic Code Optimizer For Polyhedral Compilers (Merouani et al., 2024)
related_work.difference: Uses deep learning for polyhedral loop optimization. We target general-purpose data layout and use lightweight models (XGBoost) for fast compilation.

hypothesis: A learned static predictor, trained on static program features from LLVM IR, can identify profitable structure layout transformations with accuracy within 20% of profile-guided approaches, while eliminating profiling overhead entirely.

confirmed: LayoutLearner achieves within 20% of profile-guided layout optimization performance (measured by cache miss reduction)
Compilation-time overhead remains below 5% compared to -O3
Identifies profitable transformations on at least 40% of benchmark programs
Identifies some opportunities missed by RebaseDL's static analysis
refuted: Static features cannot reliably predict profitable layouts (prediction accuracy < 50%)
Performance gains are statistically insignificant (< 2% geomean speedup)

experimental_plan.expected_results: 60-80% of profile-guided optimization gains with <5% compilation overhead; some opportunities missed by RebaseDL

computational_requirements.feasibility: Feasible - adopts simulation-based approach: Python-based feature extraction from LLVM bitcode, training XGBoost models on CPU, evaluation on PolyBench and small SPEC subset. Full LLVM pass implementation deferred to future work.

novelty_claim: First work to apply learned static prediction (proven by Rotem & Cummins for branches) specifically to data layout optimization. While Rotem & Cummins showed ML can replace profiling for branch prediction, no prior work extends this to layout. We acknowledge this is a domain extension rather than methodological innovation, but data layout presents unique challenges (higher-dimensional output, context-dependency, legality constraints) that make it a distinct and harder problem.
positioning_vs_rebasedl: RebaseDL uses static analysis with fixed thresholds; we use ML to learn from data. Our approach may identify opportunities missed by RebaseDL's conservative analysis, though RebaseDL has the advantage of being fully implemented. We view the approaches as complementary—future work could combine them.
why_harder_than_branch_prediction: Higher-dimensional output space: predicting field groupings vs. binary branch decision
Context-dependent optimality: layout quality depends on surrounding code region
Legality constraints: layout errors can cause correctness issues (unlike branch prediction)
Delayed feedback: cache effects harder to measure than branch outcomes
Transformation synthesis: requires generating IR modifications, not just metadata
potential_impact: If successful, this work demonstrates that Rotem & Cummins' methodology extends to data layout, potentially enabling automatic layout optimization in production compilers without profiling overhead. Particularly valuable for: (1) applications where profiling is impractical, (2) CI/CD pipelines where profile collection is burdensome, (3) discovering layout opportunities missed by conservative static analysis.
